12. Online Learning

12.1. Online algorithms

  • Recall a competitive algorithm is -competitive for if for any sequence of requests ,

    (27)

    where OPT is the optimal offline algorithm.

  • We say a randomized algorithm is -competitive analogously by replacing left side with expectation.

  • Oblivious adversaries design the entire sequence in advance knowing but not the outcomes of any of the random coins tossed by . An adaptive adversary does it online.

    • Note.If the adversary is adaptive it is not clear whether OPT should be online or offline. For oblivious adversaries OPT is taken as offline.

12.2. Surveying competitive problems

12.2.1. Self-organizing lists

  • In Lecture 5 on competitive analysis, we considered the move-to-front (MTF) algorithm for self-organizing lists. We showed that MTF is 4-competitive. We can modify the model a little, where each access to the current list of an element still costs , but immediately after accessing , the algorithm can optionally move to the front of L for free. We then showed that in this cost model, MTF is 2-competitive. Let’s look at a further modified model, where the optional free move allows the algorithm to optionally move x anywhere front of its current position in L for free. In this cost model too, MTF is 2-competitive. Let’s call this cost model the FREE-MOVE-AHEAD model.
  • Randomized algo for self-organizing lists

12.3. Online Learning Algorithms

Consider a supervised learner against an adaptive adversary. Learning is able to choose current action.

  • is number of time steps
  • is set of actions
  • is cost of action . WLOG.
  • is the distribution over at time

Online learning algorithm: at time , the learner picks , adversary picks , and then the learner picks according to and then learns .

Over time steps, the expected loss incurred by the learning

(28)

We benchmark the learning’s policy using regret. Let be a benchmark. Then the learner has vanishing / no regret if

(29)

A reasonable benchmark is to choose the best action in hindsight. But it is impossible for an online algorithm to achieve no regret wrt this benchmark. A more reasonable one is to to choose the best fixed action in hindsight. Regret wrt this benchmark is called external regret.

Proposition 3.

  • It is impossible for an online algorithm to achieve no regret wrt first benchmark.
  • It is impossible for a deterministic online algorithm to achieve vanishining external regret.
Proof

12.4. [COOL] Example: Multiplicative weights

Consider a learning with access to experts who at each time step output a prediction of either or . The learner initializes weights equal to 1 for each expert. Each time step, the learner takes a weighted average of their predictions, and outputs the weighted majority. Then we update each weight to be multiplied by for each incorrect expert, where is a parameter. THe external regret is based on the best expert (the one who makes the least mistakes).

Proposition 4. The deterministic algorithm cannot always achieve vanishing external regret for
Proof

Suppose we make a total of mistakes and the best expert makes a total of mistakes. Each time a learner makes a mistake, at least half of the experts were wrong (since we initialized weights to 1). Hence if we make a mistake at time step , then

Hence if we make a total of mistakes over time steps, then . Suppose the best expert made mistakes over the time steps. Then since , we have

(30)

Taking logarithms and using the fact that for , we have

(31)

Hence the external regret is

(32)

It is possible for as may grow with .

□

A randomized version can achieve vanishing regret. The idea is to sample an expert according to their weights and use their prediction.

Derive this equation, where is

Let be the random variable denoting the number of mistakes. With all the weights initialized to 1, we have

(33)

This yields .

Hence the algorithm can achieve vanishing external regret by taking arbitrarily lcose to zero.

For non-binary decision making, the resulting algorithm is called HEDGE ([TODO] Recitation 10)
